answers.value:
When I initially enrolled in the Doctor of Civil Engineering Sciences programme in October 2024, my intention was to pursue a very research-oriented academic trajectory focusing on probabilistic modelling of climate-resilient coastal structures. Over the past year, however, it has become increasingly clear—both to me and to my supervisor, Prof. Dr. ir. Tom De Smet—that the core of my work, my interests, and the external collaborations I am building are significantly more practice- and application-oriented than what is typically expected in the Civil Engineering Sciences doctorate.

My current research focuses on the development, field validation, and implementation of sensor-based monitoring systems for ageing quay walls and lock complexes in Flemish inland ports. In practice, this means I spend a substantial amount of my time on:
- Designing and prototyping low-cost sensor modules (strain gauges, accelerometers, corrosion sensors) that can withstand harsh marine environments.
- Integrating these modules with industrial IoT platforms and data acquisition hardware used by port authorities and engineering firms.
- Developing practical guidelines and standard operating procedures for maintenance crews and infrastructure managers who will use the monitoring data in their day-to-day decision-making.

Over the course of several joint meetings with our industrial partners (notably De Vlaamse Waterweg nv and the Port of Ghent technical services), it became apparent that the expectations for the PhD output include not only scientific publications but also validated prototypes, implementation manuals, and training sessions for technical staff. These deliverables, as well as the strongly design- and implementation-focused nature of my daily work, align much more closely with the objectives, learning outcomes, and evaluation criteria of the Doctor of Engineering Technology programme within our faculty.

In concrete terms, the main reasons for requesting this switch are:

1. **Better alignment of programme outcomes with my research activities**  
   The Doctor of Engineering Technology emphasises the translation of research into concrete technological solutions and industrial applications. My current project is embedded in a long-term collaboration with industry partners who explicitly request deployable tools and demonstrators. For example, during the pilot installation in the Kluizendok quay wall in November 2025, I personally coordinated the on-site installation with a team of technicians, adapted the sensor housing to fit existing reinforcement layouts, and wrote a step-by-step installation protocol that is now being used as an internal reference document by De Vlaamse Waterweg. These kinds of activities—prototyping, field testing, and technology transfer—are central within Engineering Technology but more peripheral within Civil Engineering Sciences.

2. **Coherence with my academic and professional background**  
   I obtained a Master of Science in Electromechanical Engineering Technology (KU Leuven, 2023) with a strong focus on mechatronics and embedded systems. When I started the PhD, I was very motivated by the civil engineering application domain (ports and waterways), but in practice my strengths and responsibilities lie in system integration, firmware development for microcontrollers, and practical testing. For instance, the core innovation of my current work is a modular sensor node architecture with edge computing capabilities that can locally filter and compress vibration data before transmission, which significantly reduces data storage and bandwidth requirements for continuous quay wall monitoring. This type of engineering design work is more naturally evaluated and supported within the Engineering Technology framework.

3. **Supervisory and research group fit**  
   My main supervisor, Prof. De Smet, is affiliated with both the Civil Engineering and the Engineering Technology programmes, and he has indicated that my project is more in line with the typical portfolio of the Engineering Technology doctoral candidates in his research group. Several of my closest collaborators in the lab (e.g. two postdoctoral researchers and another PhD student) are enrolled in the Engineering Technology doctorate, and our work is highly integrated: we share code repositories, hardware designs, and experimental setups. From a practical standpoint, it would make supervision, evaluation, and internal progress monitoring much more coherent if we are all evaluated under the same doctoral framework.

4. **Coursework, doctoral training, and career perspective**  
   In the past academic year, I have followed several doctoral schools courses that are particularly targeted at practice-oriented research and technology transfer (e.g. "Design of Reliable Embedded Systems for Harsh Environments", "From Prototype to Product in Industrial Collaborations", and "IP Management and Valorisation in Engineering"). I also co-supervise two bachelor thesis projects in Industrial Engineering Technology that involve implementing and testing my sensor modules in a scaled laboratory setup. The feedback I have received from these teaching and collaboration activities suggests that my profile is strongly aligned with an Engineering Technology career path, where I can combine applied research with industrial partnerships and education. Switching to the Doctor of Engineering Technology will thus provide a more consistent profile for my future academic or R&D career, whether I remain in academia or move to an industrial research centre.

5. **Agreement with supervisor and continuity of the research project**  
   This request has been discussed extensively with my supervisor during our weekly meetings from September to December 2025. We jointly reviewed the doctoral regulations and the differences between the two programmes. We concluded that:
   - The research topic, funding, and supervisory arrangement will remain unchanged.
   - My current progress (including one submitted journal paper, one accepted conference paper, and the completed field trial at Kluizendok) can be fully integrated into the Engineering Technology doctoral trajectory.
   - The expected final outcomes—validated monitoring system, practical implementation guidelines, and a software toolkit for data analysis—are better captured in the Engineering Technology evaluation criteria.

In sum, this switch does not represent a change in my research topic, supervisor, or faculty, but rather a formal adjustment so that the doctoral programme accurately reflects the applied, design-oriented, and implementation-focused nature of my work. I am convinced that enrolling in the Doctor of Engineering Technology will ensure a more appropriate academic framing of my research, a more coherent set of expectations for my doctoral output, and a better alignment with my background and long-term professional ambitions.
